1: וַיָּגָר בִּגְרָר (wayyāḡār biḡrār) — "he sojourned in Gerar." Gûr (to sojourn, dwell as a resident alien) — the same verb that defines Abraham's entire existence in Canaan (Genesis 17:8; Genesis 21:23; Genesis 26:3; Genesis 28:4). Gerar is a Philistine city in the Negev region (between Kadesh and Shur) — the territory of a settled people with their own political structure. The narrative repeats the wife-sister motif of Genesis 12 in a new setting; this is the second of the three iterations (Genesis 12:10-20; here; Genesis 26:6-11 with Isaac and Rebekah).
2: אֲחֹתִי הִוא (ʾăḥōtî hîʾ, she is my sister) — the same deception formula as Genesis 12:13, now enacted in Philistine territory. Aḇîmelek melek-gĕrār (Abimelech king of Gerar) — Aḇîmelek = "my father is king" (a throne name or hereditary title in the region; used for multiple Philistine rulers: cf. Genesis 26:1; Ps 34 superscript). וַיִּשְׁלַח אֲבִימֶלֶךְ — sent for Sarah: the king's acquisition of a woman for his household is a royal prerogative in the ANE; the danger to the covenant line is immediate and concrete.
3: וַיָּבֹא אֱלֹהִים אֶל-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ בַּחֲלוֹם הַלָּיְלָה — "God came to Abimelech in a dream of the night." The divine name throughout Genesis 20 is ʾĔlōhîm (God), not YHWH — the generic Semitic divine name rather than the covenant name. This is consistent with the chapter's setting in a non-Israelite context: God is revealing himself to a Philistine king through the form of divine communication available to all peoples (dreams — cf. Gen 41; Dan 2). הִנְּךָ מֵת (hinnĕḵā mēt, you are as good as dead) — the participial construction (hinneḵā + participle) expresses an imminent or already-determined state: "behold you [are] dead." The death-sentence is delivered before any wrongdoing has occurred, emphasizing that God's intervention precedes Abimelech's potential sin.
4: הֲגוֹי גַּם-צַדִּיק תַּהֲרֹג (hăgôy gam-ṣaddîq taharōḡ) — "will you destroy even a righteous nation?" Abimelech uses ʾAḏōnāy (Lord/Sovereign) — the Hebrew honorific used by Israelites before YHWH, suggesting that the narrator considers Abimelech's recognition of God as genuine. Ṣaddîq (righteous, in the right) — Abimelech claims moral innocence, using the covenant-justice vocabulary (ṣĕḏāqāh field of meaning). The question echoes Abraham's own challenge to divine justice in Genesis 18:25 ("will the judge of all the earth not do justice?") — a pagan king makes the same moral argument as the covenant patriarch.
5: בְּתָם-לְבָבִי וּבְנִקְיֹן כַּפַּי (bĕtom-lĕbābî ûbĕniqyōn kappāy) — "with the integrity of my heart and the cleanness of my hands." Tōm (wholeness, integrity) of the heart and niqyōn (cleanness, innocence) of the hands — the combination appears in Psalms 24:4 (the worshipper who may ascend YHWH's mountain) and Psalms 26:6; the moral idiom of clean hands/pure heart is a covenant-piety formula applied here by a non-Israelite king. Abimelech's self-defense is complete and legitimate — he acted on the information given.
6: גַּם אָנֹכִי יָדַעְתִּי כִּי בְתָם-לְבָבְךָ עָשִׂיתָ זֹּאת — "Yes, I myself also knew that you did this with the integrity of your heart." God confirms Abimelech's innocence — divine omniscience vindicates the claim. וָאֶחְשֹׂךְ גַּם-אָנֹכִי אוֹתְךָ מֵחֲטוֹ-לִי — "and I myself also withheld you from sinning against me." Ḥāśaḵ (to withhold, restrain, hold back) — God actively prevented Abimelech from sinning by means of the physical situation (not letting him touch Sarah). The divine protection operates at the providential level: God manages the circumstances to prevent the sin, then warns the king to reverse the situation.
7: כִּי-נָבִיא הוּא (kî-nāḇîʾ hûʾ, for he is a prophet) — the first and only time Abraham is explicitly called a nāḇîʾ (prophet) in Genesis. Nāḇîʾ — the Hebrew term for prophet (from nābaʾ, to speak as a mouthpiece; or possibly related to Akkadian nabû, to call/name). The prophetic office is here defined functionally: the prophet intercedes (יִתְפַּלֵּל, Hithpael of pālal, to pray/intercede) on behalf of others. Abraham's prophetic role is intercessory — he will pray for Abimelech's household (Genesis 20:17-18). This is the OT's first connection between prophecy and intercession.
8: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אֲבִימֶלֶךְ בַּבֹּקֶר — "Abimelech rose early in the morning." Hiškîm (Hiphil, to rise early) — the same word used for Abraham's early rising in Genesis 22:3 (for the Akedah) and Genesis 21:14 (to send Hagar away). The urgency of rising early marks responsiveness to divine word. Wayyiqrāʾ lĕḵol-ʿăḇāḏāyw (he summoned all his servants) — the king immediately marshals his household officials to deal with the divine threat, indicating he takes the dream-warning with complete seriousness.
9: מַה-עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ (mah-ʿāśîtā lānû) — "what have you done to us?" Abimelech's reproach mirrors exactly the formula of divine judicial inquiry (Genesis 3:13; Genesis 4:10; Genesis 12:18) — and Pharaoh's rebuke in 12:18 used the same words. מַעֲשִׂים אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יֵעָשׂוּ (maʿăśîm ʾăšer lōʾ-yēʿāśû) — "deeds that should not be done." The Niphal passive construction with lōʾ creates a categorical prohibition: these are actions that lie outside the bounds of acceptable conduct. The pagan king invokes a moral absolute — a recognition of universal ethical norms independent of covenant. The reproach is the same as in Genesis 12:18-19: the moral credibility of the covenant family is implicated.
10: מָה רָאִיתָ כִּי עָשִׂיתָ אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה — "what did you see, that you did this thing?" Rāʾāh (to see, perceive) in the sense of: what assessment, what observation of the situation led you to this action? The question asks for the underlying reasoning — not just what happened but why. Abraham's answer in Genesis 20:11-13 is the longest self-defense in the patriarchal narratives, revealing his theological reasoning and domestic arrangement.
11: אַךְ אֵין-יִרְאַת אֱלֹהִים בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה — "surely there is no fear of God in this place." Yirʾat ʾĔlōhîm (fear of God) — the phrase that in Proverbs 1:7 is "the beginning of wisdom" and that defines the morally ordered community; Abraham assumed that without YHWH-worship there would be no moral restraint. The narrative immediately subverts his assumption: Abimelech shows more moral sensitivity than Abraham. The patriarchal character is presented without sanitizing — Abraham's fear-based reasoning led to a deceptive action that placed his wife in danger.
12: וְגַם-אָמְנָה אֲחֹתִי בַת-אָבִי הִוא אַךְ לֹא בַת-אִמִּי — "and indeed she is truly my sister, daughter of my father, though not daughter of my mother." The half-sibling relationship between Abraham and Sarah (same father, different mothers) is revealed here for the first time. This makes the deception technically a half-truth — she is his half-sister — while functionally deceptive since she is also his wife. The law of Leviticus 18:9; 20:17 would prohibit this marriage, but the patriarchal narrative precedes the Sinai legislation and presents the situation without anachronistic condemnation.
13: כַּאֲשֶׁר הִתְעוּ אֹתִי אֱלֹהִים מִבֵּית אָבִי — "when God caused me to wander from my father's household." Tāʿāh (Hiphil: to cause to wander, lead astray) — with God as subject, this is not a negative but a description of the nomadic calling: God led Abraham out in a wandering way. The same verb in Psalms 107:4 ("they wandered in the desert wasteland") describes Israel's wilderness sojourn as divine leading-through-wandering. Abraham's self-account frames the journey as divine leading; the wife-sister agreement was a precaution for that wandering. עַשְׂי חַסְדֵּךְ (ʿaśî ḥasdêḵ, do your covenant-loyalty/kindness to me) — ḥeseḏ (covenant loyalty, steadfast love) as the basis of the domestic agreement.
14: וַיִּקַּח אֲבִימֶלֶךְ צֹאן וּבָקָר וַעֲבָדִים וּשְׁפָחֹת — Abimelech's restitution mirrors Pharaoh's gifts in Genesis 12:16 — the same categories (sheep and cattle, male and female slaves). The parallel is deliberate: both pagan rulers respond to the divine threat by enriching Abraham; the material wealth of the covenant family grows through these encounters. The return of Sarah is the primary act: the goods are accompaniment to restoration of the wife.
15: הִנֵּה אַרְצִי לְפָנֶיךָ (hinnēh ʾarṣî lĕpānêḵā, behold my land is before you) — Abimelech offers Abraham open access to his territory; the formula "the land is before you" echoes Abraham's offer to Lot in 13:9 and the covenant land-promise (cf. Deuteronomy 1:8; Genesis 4:1). Bĕṭôḇ bĕʿênêḵā šēḇ (wherever is good in your eyes, dwell) — the idiom of personal choice and sovereign hospitality; Abimelech grants Abraham the right to settle anywhere in Gerar's territory.
16: הִנֵּה הוּא-לְךָ כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם (hinnēh hûʾ-lĕḵā kĕsût ʿênāyim, behold it is for you a covering of eyes) — the thousand shekels of silver is described as a "covering of eyes." The phrase kĕsût ʿênāyim is rare and its precise meaning is debated: (1) something that averts others' eyes from the offense — a payment to make people overlook what happened; (2) a face-veil or covering for Sarah's honor — restoring her dignity in the eyes of the community; (3) compensation for the "unveiled" or "uncovered" offense. The LXX reads "this shall be for you for the honor of your face" (τοῦτό σοι εἰς τιμήν). Whatever the precise idiom, the silver is restorative: וְנֹכַחַת לְכֹל — "you are fully vindicated/set right before all."
17: וַיִּתְפַּלֵּל אַבְרָהָם אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים — "Abraham prayed to God." Hitpallēl (Hithpael of pālal, to intercede, pray) — the prophetic intercessory role announced in Genesis 20:7 is now fulfilled. Abraham prays for the pagan king's household — one of the clearest instances of the OT's intercessory prayer on behalf of non-Israelites. וַיִּרְפָּא אֱלֹהִים אֶת-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ — "and God healed Abimelech." Rāpāʾ (to heal) — healing as the restoration of divine blessing; the wombs closed by God's judgment (Genesis 20:18) are reopened by God's grace through prophetic intercession.
18: כִּי-עָצֹר עָצַר יְהוָה בְּעַד כָּל-רֶחֶם (kî-ʿāṣōr ʿāṣar YHWH bĕʿaḏ kol-reḥem) — "for the LORD had tightly closed (infinitive absolute + verb) every womb." The emphatic infinitive absolute ʿāṣōr ʿāṣar (surely shut up, firmly closed) uses the same verb as 16:2 where Sarai says "the LORD has shut me up from bearing." The closing of the Abimelech household's wombs is YHWH's name for the first time in the chapter — the narrator reveals that the divine action protecting Sarah was specifically YHWH even in this Elohim-narrative. The irony: the patriarch whose wife is barren temporarily makes others barren by his deception.
